**Emerich Vogl** (12 August 1905 – 29 October 1971) was a [Romanian](/wiki/Romania "Romania") football player and coach of [Banat Swabian](/wiki/Banat_Swabian "Banat Swabian") ethnicity, who was a member of the [Romania national team](/wiki/Romania_national_football_team "Romania national football team") that participated in the [1930 World Cup](/wiki/1930_FIFA_World_Cup "1930 FIFA World Cup") in [Uruguay](/wiki/Uruguay "Uruguay") and the [1934 edition](/wiki/1934_FIFA_World_Cup "1934 FIFA World Cup") in [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy").

## Club career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Emerich_Vogl&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Club career")]

Vogl was born on 12 August 1905 in [Temesvár](/wiki/Timi%C8%99oara "Timișoara"), [Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary"), growing up in the [Mehala](/wiki/Mehala "Mehala") neighborhood, then playing as a youth for local club [Chinezul](/wiki/Chinezul_Timi%C8%99oara "Chinezul Timișoara") between 1921 and 1922.[[1]](#cite_note-RS-1)[[3]](#cite_note-tiki-4)[[4]](#cite_note-5) In 1922, at age 17, Vogl made his debut for the senior team and until 1924 he played on different positions, before settling as a central midfielder.[[3]](#cite_note-tiki-4) He played for Chinezul until 1929, winning five [Divizia A](/wiki/Liga_I "Liga I") titles with them.[[1]](#cite_note-RS-1)[[5]](#cite_note-RSC-6)[[6]](#cite_note-adevarul-7) For the [1925–26](/wiki/1925%E2%80%9326_Divizia_A "1925–26 Divizia A") title, he scored 15 goals in the regional and national championship combined, being the team's second top-scorer after [Augustin Semler](/wiki/Augustin_Semler "Augustin Semler") who scored 41.[[1]](#cite_note-RS-1)[[5]](#cite_note-RSC-6)[[6]](#cite_note-adevarul-7) Around this time he was considered by the press to be the best central midfielder in Europe, being appreciated for his technical abilities and his physical condition, at one point winning a local [800 metres](/wiki/800_metres "800 metres") running competition.[[3]](#cite_note-tiki-4)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/9/9b/Emerich_Vogl%2C_Los_Sports%2C_1930-08-01_%28386%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Emerich_Vogl,_Los_Sports,_1930-08-01_(386).jpg)

Vogl in the Chilean sports magazine *[Los Sports](/wiki/Los_Sports "Los Sports")* in 1930

In 1929, together with his teammate [László Raffinsky](/wiki/L%C3%A1szl%C3%B3_Raffinsky "László Raffinsky") he went to [Juventus București](/wiki/FC_Petrolul_Ploie%C8%99ti "FC Petrolul Ploiești").[[1]](#cite_note-RS-1)[[3]](#cite_note-tiki-4)[[6]](#cite_note-adevarul-7) Vogl made his debut for them under coach [Gyula Feldmann](/wiki/Gyula_Feldmann "Gyula Feldmann") on 13 October in a 1–0 home victory against [Venus București](/wiki/FC_Venus_Bucure%C8%99ti "FC Venus București") in the regional championship, scoring his first goal on 3 November in a 1–0 away win over [Maccabi București](/wiki/Maccabi_Bucure%C8%99ti "Maccabi București").[[7]](#cite_note-8) Juventus won the regional championship, thus qualifying for the [national championship](/wiki/1929%E2%80%9330_Divizia_A "1929–30 Divizia A") which was won after a 3–0 victory in the final against [Gloria Arad](/wiki/CS_Gloria_Arad "CS Gloria Arad").[[1]](#cite_note-RS-1)[[3]](#cite_note-tiki-4)[[5]](#cite_note-RSC-6)[[6]](#cite_note-adevarul-7)[[8]](#cite_note-ploiestichampionship-9) Vogl was being used by coaches Feldmann and [György Hlavay](/wiki/Gy%C3%B6rgy_Hlavay "György Hlavay") in all 14 matches during the campaign, having one goal scored.[[1]](#cite_note-RS-1)[[5]](#cite_note-RSC-6)[[8]](#cite_note-ploiestichampionship-9) In the following seven years, Vogl appeared regularly for the team, and helped it gain promotion to [Divizia A](/wiki/Liga_I "Liga I") by the end of the 1932–33 season.[[1]](#cite_note-RS-1)[[9]](#cite_note-ploiesti-10)[[10]](#cite_note-ploiesti2-11)[[11]](#cite_note-ploiesti3-12)[[12]](#cite_note-ploiesti4-13) He also had two spells as a player-coach for them.[[9]](#cite_note-ploiesti-10)[[10]](#cite_note-ploiesti2-11)[[13]](#cite_note-ploiesti5-14)[[14]](#cite_note-ploiesti6-15) The highlights of these years were a third place in the [1935–36](/wiki/1935%E2%80%9336_Divizia_A "1935–36 Divizia A") season and reaching two times in a row the [Cupa României](/wiki/Cupa_Rom%C3%A2niei "Cupa României") semi-finals.[[1]](#cite_note-RS-1)[[15]](#cite_note-16)

## International career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Emerich_Vogl&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: International career")]

Vogl played 29 games and scored one goal for [Romania](/wiki/Romania_national_football_team "Romania national football team").[[3]](#cite_note-tiki-4)[[6]](#cite_note-adevarul-7)[[16]](#cite_note-EU-17) He made his debut on 31 August 1924 under coach Teofil Moraru in an away friendly against [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia_national_football_team "Czechoslovakia national football team") which ended with a 4–1 loss.[[3]](#cite_note-tiki-4)[[6]](#cite_note-adevarul-7)[[16]](#cite_note-EU-17)[[17]](#cite_note-18) In his third match for the national team, Vogl was named the [team captain](/wiki/Captain_(association_football) "Captain (association football)") in a 3–1 away win in a friendly over [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey_national_football_team "Turkey national football team").[[3]](#cite_note-tiki-4)[[16]](#cite_note-EU-17) He scored his only goal in an 8–1 victory against [Greece](/wiki/Greece_national_football_team "Greece national football team") during the successful [1929–31 Balkan Cup](/wiki/1929%E2%80%9331_Balkan_Cup "1929–31 Balkan Cup").[[3]](#cite_note-tiki-4)[[6]](#cite_note-adevarul-7)[[16]](#cite_note-EU-17)[[18]](#cite_note-EU2-19)[[19]](#cite_note-EU3-20)

He was selected by coach [Constantin Rădulescu](/wiki/Constantin_R%C4%83dulescu_(footballer,_born_1896) "Constantin Rădulescu (footballer, born 1896)") to be part of the squad for the [1930 World Cup](/wiki/1930_FIFA_World_Cup "1930 FIFA World Cup"), but the chairman of Astra Română, a company where Vogl and his teammate [László Raffinsky](/wiki/L%C3%A1szl%C3%B3_Raffinsky "László Raffinsky") were [office workers](/wiki/Office_workers "Office workers"), forbade the two players from leaving their workplace.[[3]](#cite_note-tiki-4)[[6]](#cite_note-adevarul-7)[[20]](#cite_note-agenda-21)[[21]](#cite_note-22) Eventually, after the [Romanian Football Federation](/wiki/Romanian_Football_Federation "Romanian Football Federation")'s secretary Octav Luchide went to the company with a letter from [King Carol II](/wiki/King_Carol_II "King Carol II"), they were allowed to participate.[[3]](#cite_note-tiki-4)[[6]](#cite_note-adevarul-7)[[20]](#cite_note-agenda-21) Vogl played in both Group 3 matches at that World Cup, a 3–1 win over [Peru](/wiki/Peru_national_football_team "Peru national football team") and a 4–0 loss to hosts and eventual world champions [Uruguay](/wiki/Uruguay_national_football_team "Uruguay national football team").[[16]](#cite_note-EU-17) Afterwards he helped the team win two competitions.[[16]](#cite_note-EU-17)[[22]](#cite_note-EU4-23)[[23]](#cite_note-EU5-24) The first was the [1933 Balkan Cup](/wiki/1933_Balkan_Cup "1933 Balkan Cup") where he appeared in all three matches and the second was the [1931–34 Central European Cup for Amateurs](/wiki/1931%E2%80%931934_Central_European_Cup_for_Amateurs "1931–1934 Central European Cup for Amateurs") where he played five games.[[16]](#cite_note-EU-17)[[22]](#cite_note-EU4-23)[[23]](#cite_note-EU5-24) Vogl played two games in the successful [1934 World Cup qualifiers](/wiki/1934_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification "1934 FIFA World Cup qualification"), being selected by coaches [Josef Uridil](/wiki/Josef_Uridil "Josef Uridil") and Rădulescu to be part of the [final tournament](/wiki/1934_FIFA_World_Cup "1934 FIFA World Cup") squad.[[3]](#cite_note-tiki-4)[[16]](#cite_note-EU-17) There, he played and captained the team for the last time in a first round exit as they were defeated with 2–1 by eventual finalists, Czechoslovakia.[[3]](#cite_note-tiki-4)[[16]](#cite_note-EU-17)

## Managerial career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Emerich_Vogl&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Managerial career")]

Vogl's first coaching experience was when he replaced [Rudolf Wetzer](/wiki/Rudolf_Wetzer "Rudolf Wetzer") at [Juventus București](/wiki/FC_Petrolul_Ploie%C8%99ti "FC Petrolul Ploiești") for the second half of the 1930–31 regional championship, being a player-coach.[[3]](#cite_note-tiki-4)[[6]](#cite_note-adevarul-7)[[9]](#cite_note-ploiesti-10)[[10]](#cite_note-ploiesti2-11) He took the club from the third place and finished in second with only one point below winners [Unirea Tricolor București](/wiki/Unirea_Tricolor_Bucure%C8%99ti "Unirea Tricolor București").[[9]](#cite_note-ploiesti-10) In the following season he finished in fourth place.[[10]](#cite_note-ploiesti2-11) Then in the 1932–33 edition he led them in the first half and finished first, but was replaced with [Ladislau Csillag](/wiki/Ladislau_Csillag "Ladislau Csillag") for the second half who kept the team on the lead position, earning the promotion to [Divizia A](/wiki/Liga_I "Liga I").[[11]](#cite_note-ploiesti3-12)[[12]](#cite_note-ploiesti4-13) He was player-coach again when he replaced Csillag for the second half of the [1933–34](/wiki/1933%E2%80%9334_Divizia_A "1933–34 Divizia A") season, making his Divizia A debut in a 3–1 home win over [Șoimii Sibiu](/wiki/%C8%98oimii_Sibiu "Șoimii Sibiu"), finishing the season in fourth place.[[13]](#cite_note-ploiesti5-14) After finishing the first half of the [following season](/wiki/1934%E2%80%9335_Divizia_A "1934–35 Divizia A") in 10th place from 12 teams, Vogl was replaced with Gyula Dobo.[[14]](#cite_note-ploiesti6-15)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Emerich_Vogl_1969.jpg/250px-Emerich_Vogl_1969.jpg)](/wiki/File:Emerich_Vogl_1969.jpg)

Vogl in 1969

In 1943 he led Juventus once again, this time only as coach, competing due to [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") in an unofficial championship and in the [Cupa României](/wiki/1942%E2%80%9343_Cupa_Rom%C3%A2niei "1942–43 Cupa României").[[24]](#cite_note-25) On 13 June 1943, he led [Romania's national team](/wiki/Romania_national_football_team "Romania national football team") for the first time in a 2–2 friendly draw against [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia_national_football_team "Slovakia national football team").[[25]](#cite_note-EU6-26)[[26]](#cite_note-27) Vogl returned to Juventus for a final time in the second half of the 1945–46 București championship, replacing [Nicolae Petrescu](/wiki/Nicolae_Petrescu_(footballer) "Nicolae Petrescu (footballer)") who remained in his staff as an assistant.[[27]](#cite_note-28) In the following two seasons he coached the team in Divizia A, finishing the first one in fourth place and the second in fifth.[[28]](#cite_note-29) However, in the [1948–49](/wiki/1948%E2%80%9349_Divizia_A "1948–49 Divizia A") season after losing in the 19th round with 3–2 a home game to [ASA București](/wiki/FCSB "FCSB") he was replaced with [Colea Vâlcov](/wiki/Colea_V%C3%A2lcov "Colea Vâlcov").[[29]](#cite_note-30) In 1949, Vogl had his second experience at Romania's national team, this time leading them together with [Coloman Braun-Bogdan](/wiki/Coloman_Braun-Bogdan "Coloman Braun-Bogdan") in a 1–1 home friendly draw against [Albania](/wiki/Albania_national_football_team "Albania national football team").[[16]](#cite_note-EU-17)[[30]](#cite_note-31) From 1950 until 1952, Vogl had his last tenure as Romania's coach, leading them in four friendly games which consisted of a win and two draws against [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia_national_football_team "Czechoslovakia national football team"), and a win over [Albania](/wiki/Albania_national_football_team "Albania national football team").[[25]](#cite_note-EU6-26) Across all of his three spells at the national team he obtained two victories, four draws and no losses.[[25]](#cite_note-EU6-26)

Between 1963 and 1967, Vogl was a consultant for [Rapid București](/wiki/FC_Rapid_Bucure%C8%99ti "FC Rapid București"), helping it win its first title in the [1966–67](/wiki/1966%E2%80%9367_Divizia_A "1966–67 Divizia A") season.[[3]](#cite_note-tiki-4)[[6]](#cite_note-adevarul-7) Afterwards he was a consultant for the national team from 1967 until 1971, helping Romania to qualify in [1970](/wiki/1970_FIFA_World_Cup "1970 FIFA World Cup") for a [FIFA World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup") after 32 years.[[3]](#cite_note-tiki-4)[[6]](#cite_note-adevarul-7)

## Death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Emerich_Vogl&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Death")]

Vogl died on 29 October 1971 at the age of 66.[[3]](#cite_note-tiki-4)
